title: LEFT JOIN
description: Keep everything on the left, even without a match.

slides.title: The Silent Users 😶
slides.content:
# The INNER problem

What if we actually *want* Bob to appear in our resulting list, even if he hasn't ordered anything yet?

If we use INNER JOIN, Bob vanishes. We need a looser, friendlier join that forgives missing data.

slides.title: The LEFT JOIN 👈
slides.content:
## Guaranteeing the left side

A **`LEFT JOIN`** guarantees that EVERY single row in the "Left" table (the table you write first in `FROM`) will survive and appear in the final grid, no matter what.

`SELECT * FROM users LEFT JOIN orders ON ...`

quizData.question: If you use a LEFT JOIN starting with `FROM users`, what is absolutely guaranteed?

options.text: Every user will be returned, even if they have zero matches in the orders table
Only users who have legally ordered items will be returned
Only orders without users will legitimately exist

quizData.explanation: A LEFT JOIN essentially says: 'Regardless of anything else, output everything from the Left (first) table. If it has matches in the Right table, cool. If not, still output it.'

slides.title: Filling with NULLs 👻
slides.content:
## Empty spaces

If Bob makes it into the final result because of the LEFT JOIN, but he has no order... what does the database put in his `order_date` column?

The database literally just inserts a **`NULL`** (blank) value for all the missing right-side order columns!

quizData.question: If a user has no matching orders during a LEFT JOIN, what populates the 'order_price' column for that returned row?

options.text: It is filled with a NULL value

quizData.explanation: Since Bob has no order, the database pads all the Order-specific columns with a NULL for his specific row.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Get all the `customers`, even if they have zero `complaints`. Join them on their `id` vs the complaint's `customer_id`.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT *
FROM customers
LEFT JOIN complaints 
  ON customers.id = complaints.customer_id;
